speaker: interviewer
text: It is November 17, 1995. The survivor is Freda Weiss. This is tape four. Please continue.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. And then she, that lady, sat me down near the table. And she made me a coffee, first time in-- in-- in over a year. She took a piece of bread and butter. And she gave it to me, to eat and to drink. When I reached the cup of coffee, I spilled it. I couldn't hold it. My hand was tremble-- you know, I was very weak. And I couldn't believe it. I was in shock that I have coffee, bread, and butter. And I start crying for happiness that I'm already in a house where I see normal people. And when I cried, she came. And she kissed my cheeks. And she said, don't cry anymore, darling. Don't cry. You are close to liberation. The Tommy-- Tommy means England and America, and Russia. Tommy means the Allies-- are not far from here. And you're going to be liberated. And you're going to get together with your mother and father and sisters. When she says mother and sisters, I cried again very, very-- I couldn't-- I couldn't just stop. I was hysterical. I says to her, I saw my mother in crematorium. I saw where they burned thousands and thousands of the people. I saw the skeletons in the-- in-- in the crematorium. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And she start crying with me. And those Russian girls, very beautiful, two girls, they were captured from the Nazis because they helped out the Jews. And they catch them. And they took them along with the Jews. Very wonderful two girls. Gentiles.

speaker: interviewer
text: How long were you there before the Americans came and liberated?

speaker: interviewee
text: In that house?

speaker: interviewer
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewee
text: Five weeks.

speaker: interviewer
text: Five weeks?

speaker: interviewee
text: That's it. After one-- yeah, and then-- well, after that, it was late in the night, she put a radio in the bedroom. The girls-- the two Russian girls got together in one bed. They gave me the other bed to sleep. And they told me don't worry. You have food, enough, because we brought food. And they couldn't take away, from us, the food. And we heard the news, what's going on, that Russia's here, and America is there, and so on. But my heart was very wounded. My heart-- I knew that-- who knows if I find some-- I knew that mother and sister, Sarah and Etu, will not be alive. That-- because I saw where they went. We were there. In the middle of the night, in the same night, the lady-- the house lady-- wake-- woke us up. Come. We come down. Three men was brought in. What German-- democratic German people find them on the streets. They put them in the burl-- while-burrows.

speaker: interviewer
text: Wheelbarrows?

speaker: interviewee
text: Wheelbarrows. They put them in the wheelbarrows. And they brought them in mid-- middle of the night, already almost in the morning, they brought them to this woman. They were almost dead. They couldn't swallow. They couldn't eat. They couldn't talk. They were all pus running from them. They were almost dead. She had hidden a doctor, a Czech doctor, not far. She called him up to come, right away, over. The doctor came over. She waked us-- woked us up from sleep, we should help the doctor, give him the cotton, to clean up those three people.

speaker: interviewee
text: They gave them those food under the-- not in the mouth, under the [? lip, ?] you know? To-- nourishing, for the nourish. And those people got better. They gave them injections to get better. And we, the three girls, helped out there.

speaker: interviewee
text: And two, three days later, those three men was alive. Beautiful. They took a shower. And they were gorgeous young men. One was a lawyer, two doctors from Amsterdam, Holland. And the-- and the doctor was-- two doctors from Amsterdam. And the lawyer was from Prague, Czechoslovakia.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: And they told their stories, I told my story. We talked. And we-- but-- and five weeks later, we were together there, by this family.

speaker: interviewee
text: You know what she did? She wanted us to see the-- the outside. Because she hidded us. God forbid if other Germans would know.

speaker: interviewee
text: She put us-- fur coats on us, on the three girls. She gave us beautiful hats. And she make-upped us. And she took us in-- in-- in a garden, in a park, that we should have air, and we should see the world. Because she didn't know yet how long it will take until the Allies will liberate us. And that was was five weeks after that. She comes in to me, say, Freda, Freda, and she kisses me. She hugs me. It came, your day, that you going to be liberated. I have a flag for you. She made the blue-white flag for me here. And she pinned it on my dress. And for the other Czech and Holland, everybody, their own state. And I went out from the house. Because they are coming with tanks, and with the vans, and out, you know. And the American are coming. The American are coming. My God, I didn't know not one word, English. I-- In fact, I speak so many languages, but not English. I came out and waited in the end of the street like that, and waited to look. And then I see people are coming and coming. And I-- and I waved, waved with  my hand. And they waved to me. But all of a sudden, a tank stops near me. A beautiful young man comes out from the tank and comes to me and embraces me. [? Meydl, ?] [? meydl-- ?] in Jewish, he said.

speaker: interviewee
text: He saw-- [INAUDIBLE] Yiddish, [? meydl ?] little girl-- no. Girl, girl, you are Jewish. I see the flag. I says yes, who else am I, if I come from the concentration camps? And crying, he cries. And I cry. Whom do you have? And what you doing? You need something? I want to help you. I said, the biggest help will be that you should help those people here, those German people, democratic German, fine people. They gave life to me. They-- Where did you go after you were liberated?

speaker: interviewee
text: After that, he took me to stores, to get material. He took me to dressmaker, to have my dresses made.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: To-- they took me to-- to make-- to cut my hair. I had a little bit, hair already, because--

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: --I was shaved.

speaker: interviewer
text: Mhm.

speaker: interviewee
text: And he took me in an apartment, where to live. I told him, [? Josef, ?] I will not live here. I have to go and look for my dear ones. Maybe I find somebody alive.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you go back to your hometown?

speaker: interviewee
text: I went back to my hometown. I-- I went first to Prague. Prague, Czechoslovakia. I said to him, goodbye. And he gave me a picture. And he said, when you come to America, I'm an American. I live in the Bronx. And my number-- my-- my telephone number is so and so. When you come to America, you never know, he says, you look up-- you look me up. I came to America. OK, from there I will not [INAUDIBLE]-- from there I find friends, a lot of friends.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK, in Prague were you--

speaker: interviewee
text: In Prague.

speaker: interviewer
text: --were you in a displaced person camp?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was not a D-- a DP camp?

speaker: interviewer
text: A DP camp? Yes.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: For people who was just a few days.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: And then go further. I was there, and I find a lot of my friends. In fact, I find one of my boyfriends from [? Sighetu ?] [? Marmatiei, ?] [? Sighetu. ?] And he-- he wanted to marry me. But he said he wants to go to Russia to live. And I said, no, I'm not a communist. I want to go to America.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: And then we split.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I went home. And I find my brother, Menachem, who's now in Israel. And in-- in our house, where I find Menachem, there were about 25 people, young people, living together. A [NON-ENGLISH], they called it, [NON-ENGLISH], a-- a [NON-ENGLISH], all together. Menachem organized. He brought chicken. He brought food for everybody.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were these all survivors?

speaker: interviewee
text: All survivors.

speaker: interviewer
text: All survivors?

speaker: interviewee
text: Of course. They didn't find their houses. They didn't find-- [? or ?] it was occupied from the Ukrainian that were there. And after a few days we packed our things, Menachem and myself. And we, said we're going to Israel. We're going to be-- we-- and we went to [INAUDIBLE], organization who arranges to go to Israel. And we went to Budapest, Hungary. And there-- from there we went to Koblenz, that was Koblenz is Austria, already. And there was-- the England are occupied there, not the Americans. And we suffered a lot by the England there. They didn't feed us good. It wasn't like the Americans.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: And mine brother was the president of the Mizrahi. And he arranged-- he bought, in black market, a passport that somebody should go to the American zone, where the Americans are. And they going to help us. And they should take us out from here to there. And they-- and they raffled off. And it came out, my number. Everybody voted, not a raffle. They voted. And everybody voted Freda Marcus. And they gave me the passport. See, I'm blonde. I speak German. And they thought I'm-- I'm the person to go. They not going to be-- and this-- I have to go by train to Salzburg. From Koblenz to Salzburg. There is where the American occupied. I came to Salzburg with a [INAUDIBLE]. And when I came out, my husband was also there. He wasn't my husband. He was a young man. He was in the [INAUDIBLE], very religious. And I tell him, uncle-- he was very old. He's older than I am, 12 years. In that time he lost a wife with six children, by the Nazis.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where?

speaker: interviewee
text: He was married before the war.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where did your husband lose his children and his wife? In what camp?

speaker: interviewee
text: They was-- they lived in Czechoslovakia.

speaker: interviewer
text: And where did they die?

speaker: interviewee
text: In Khust. They lived in Khust. Mine husband was in-- in labor camp, about two years. But he was liberated. He came home. His wife was burned in the crematorium with six children, five boys, and one girl.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where? In Auschwitz?

speaker: interviewee
text: In Auschwitz.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: And so when he saw me he said, look, you  look so tired. Come to us. We are here, three brothers. And we have three girls, cousins. They cook for us. Come, relax. I says no, I want to help the organization in Koblenz. We should take them over to the American zone, because they suffer a lot there. He said, don't worry, I will arrange it. Just first, go and relax. You look very tired. And you are sick. I can see in your eyes. I went to his house. And the three girls was very kind to me. And since that, he didn't let me go. He would fall in love with me. And I didn't want to marry. I wanted to go to Israel very much. I had my brother, Nathan, there. And Menachem came with me. And we wanted to go to Israel. This was in 1950-- in 1945, already. And--

speaker: interviewer
text: [LAUGHS] How long were you in Austria, then, in Salzburg?

speaker: interviewee
text: In Salzburg, we were six years.

speaker: interviewer
text: Then when did you get married to your--

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewer
text: And--

speaker: interviewee
text: My husband didn't let me go. But I-- I told him, I don't-- you know, he ran after me so long, wherein I said no. And then he was very much telling me his story, about his children and his wife, that I look like his first wife and-- and I-- I was very sorry for him. He lost six children. And I thought to myself, even I'm not in love with him and I don't-- I'm going to marry him. I'm going to marry him, just that he should be happy.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I probably will be happy later on. That's what I thought to myself, like that. He lost that family. And that I can make him happy, why not?

speaker: interviewee
text: And he's a very intelligent, but too religious. I wasn't religious that time. Because I didn't-- I-- I should believe in God. Because he saved me from a lot of terrible places. I-- I could have been killed. And this is how it was. I stood. I didn't go to Israel. My brother went to Israel, Menachem. And I got married in 1946, in Salzburg. And--

speaker: interviewer
text: When did you come to America?

speaker: interviewee
text: And to America, we came in 19-- oh, 1950. Only four years, we were in Salzburg, four years. My husband was in business already, in Salzburg, exporting port. And we made-- he made a lot of money there. It was an-- organize-- organization, export coffee, and chocolate, and things like that. And imported cigarettes and things like that. And legal, legal, it was legal, allowed. Because-- and there I had the twins, boys. I had the firstborn was twins boys. And one was dead in my body three months. And no doctor knew. Or maybe they know, they didn't bother. I'm Jewish. And that was Austria, very anti-Semitic. And then I had the second. The second boy was alive, and nice and good. And I was in the hospital. And he-- I breastfeed him. And all of a sudden, the baby was crying. He had a cold, a little cold. I called in a nurse. And I told her look, the baby's running, the nose. And it cannot eat. She said, leave it to me. I will help him. She took him out from my arms. Took him in, in the room where she wanted. I don't know where, I didn't see. I didn't-- I still was so naive, that I should think, one second, maybe they going to kill him. I'm-- I didn't see that they killed him. But I have a feeling. Because the baby was OK, just the nose running. And I heard screaming from the baby, terrible, terrible. I think that they killed him. They brought back the baby maybe 15 minutes later. The baby had convulsions. And the baby was-- that had-- that had these convulsions. No, the baby didn't eat. The-- the baby was sick. They put him in, in a-- imagine, after six-- losing six children, my husband, he had a son. And born good, healthy. And all of a sudden, they come out and tell him the baby has convulsions. And the baby's not going to live. And the baby die. [CRYING] My husband had a nerve breakdown. And he was sick. I hold myself. I-- I tried not to get sick again, me too. And life was going on. That's how it is. Life goes on. And after only four years in Salzburg, maybe, mine brother-in-laws, my husband's two brothers, were here in America. They send papers. And we came out with a lot of money. My husband made very good money. When we-- and we came to America.

speaker: interviewer
text: When did you arrive in America?

speaker: interviewee
text: In 19-- when? In January. January 11, 1950.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. And did you have other children?

speaker: interviewee
text: No.

speaker: interviewer
text: After that?

speaker: interviewee
text: Only Ruthie, she should live. Oh my God.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: She's having cancer, breast cancer.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how old is Ruthie?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh. That's worse than everything. I hope she's going to be OK. I hope. I hope she's going to be. Because she's strong, and she's smart. And she laugh. She talks to me every day. And she says, Ma, don't be scared. I'm going to be OK. She talks to me like that. And that, I should say to her.

speaker: interviewer
text: When was she born? What year?

speaker: interviewee
text: In-- she's born in July. July 11, 1950.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, six months.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: She was six months when I brought her to America.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: We came with the Queen Mary, and our own expense. I never saw, in America, the HIAS or organizations who helped the Holocaust survivors. Not one penny. We had our own money. And we came here. My husband was very broken down, very old looking and very not happy. Very sad.

speaker: interviewee
text: He's a big Talmid Chacham. You know what it mean, Talmid Chacham? He had the biggest rabbi in Czechoslovakia. He was a Talmud. He was-- he learned by him. [? Rav ?] [? Dushinsky, ?] is-- was his name. It's-- it's a very brand name. You know? Like you say, brand name. Yes.

speaker: interviewee
text: And he knows a lot of Talmud. He's very smart, and in business he's very good. He's intelligent. And he said, he cannot be in New York. He cannot live in New York. It's too much noise. And he can't take it. We should go in a town to live. And he had a friend in Vineland, New Jersey, who had a chicken farm. And we went to visit him. He talked in, my husband, that he should come in the chicken farm. He should buy a chicken farm. And we would live happily ever after. He said, you're going to be very happy. It's easy life. You take workers. They work for you. Because there were Latin people working in the fields a lot. And they looked for work like that. And my husband was very happy. He said, oh, that's good. I said, no, I don't want to go in a town. I want to be in the city. And I don't want to invest in a chicken farm, my money. Your money and my money. Let's buy property. I went to look for houses. I went through property. I gave deposits. He always took back the deposits. He didn't want property. He just want a town with chicken. It's easy life. And in 1953, we were three years in New York. Spending money, the money. But we still had enough to buy a chicken farm. I didn't want. But I saw him. I always did what he wanted. Because I was sorry for him.

speaker: interviewee
text: I knew where I was and what he lost. What is money if you're not happy? So I did. I went to the chicken farm with him. It was a beautiful house, a mansion, and 10,000 chickens.

speaker: interviewee
text: And we put in the money there, with a partner. The chicken farm was over $100,000. He put in $50,000, and the partner put in $50,000. In three years' time, it's gone the money, gone the chicken, gone every-- everything.

speaker: interviewer
text: What did you do?

speaker: interviewee
text: We lost it. They had the Latin people working. And they didn't watch the chicken very, and they steal away a lot of things.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: I should--

speaker: interviewer
text: It's OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: I should be careful.

speaker: interviewer
text: It's OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: And then--

speaker: interviewer
text: Where did you go after that?

speaker: interviewee
text: And we lost-- we left the farm. I borrowed $500 from my cousin. That's what she had. And she gave it to me, the $500. I went to New York from Vineland. And I bought children's wear. A little bit, but can you-- no, they gave me $500 cash and $500 credit. They trusted me. It was people who knew us. And I went peddling in Vineland, with a car. I droved already. Second day I came  to America, I droved already. Actually, [? a ?] old car. But it was good. It took me places.

speaker: interviewee
text: I went to Jewish people, peddling, who had children, small children. And they looked over. And they say, oh, Mrs. Weiss, you have to do that?

speaker: interviewee
text: Everybody knew that we are  rich. We came with so much money. I didn't answer them. I choke. I couldn't answer. I just schlepped the car further and I went home. Without they should spend a penny for me, nothing. And I cried a lot. I cried. My life was so miserable. I had Ruthie. And then came another baby already, Reba, and-- and I left them by themselves. The partner's wife looked at them. And I saw it's no good. So I'll start peddling to Spanish people, Puerto Rican, South America, beautiful people. I'm telling you, what they did to me, God bless them. I went peddling. And one bought for 50 lei-- for 50 lei-- for $50. I think I'm on Ruskova. And they came with me, in the car. And they took me to another friend. The friend took me to another friend. And so on. I came home with money. I had the milk in the house. I had the bread. I had vegetables. I had everything. And after peddling maybe eight months, I opened a store near mine-- where I rented the apartment--

speaker: interviewer
text: And what was--

speaker: interviewee
text: It was a house.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was your store called?

speaker: interviewee
text: Freda's Clothing Store. I have cards still here, the-- you know, with mine name. I have pictures. I should take out the pictures, show you from the store, hanging them-- the dresses and the morning robes a lot. Wholesale. Wholesale and retail. But--

speaker: interviewer
text: And you took care of your family?

speaker: interviewee
text: And I took care of my family. And I went shopping. I was the buyer. And I drove the car. And I went-- and wait. When I had the store, I was so busy with customers, Spanish, most. Eh, used to come. I had beautiful things. Then when they find out-- the Gentile people, Italian, and the Jewish people-- they came to buy also, by me, retail. But South America, Brazil, Venezuela, Guatemala, all those South America cities came to buy wholesale by me. And--

speaker: interviewer
text: And how long--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah?

speaker: interviewer
text: How long did you have this store?

speaker: interviewee
text: The store I had-- one store I had five years. And after that it was too small. I find an empty garage maybe a few feet away from mine store, from the-- where I lived.

speaker: interviewee
text: I went to the man who owned. And I said, please rent me this empty place. He said, why? It's nothing. There is no roof. It's no floor. What you going to do with the four walls?

speaker: interviewee
text: Don't worry. Just give it to me. I want to make a store, a big store, like a department store. I took people. I paid them. It cost a few thousand dollars. They fixed the-- they made a roof. They made a floor. They made shelves. I took in a sewing machine. I put in a sewing machine. And I went to New York, and I shopped. Men's, ladies, and children's. The children's wear I had in the small store. And men's and ladies, and luggage and jewelry, and whatnot. Everything, whatnot, I put in the second store.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how many years did you have this until you retired?

speaker: interviewee
text: And I had-- I was 15 years in Vineland, New Jersey.

speaker: interviewer
text: And where did you go after that?

speaker: interviewee
text: And after that, Ruthie got big, to go to college. And she wanted to go to art college in New York. And I didn't want her to be alone in New York. We sold the business. We sold it. I made money. Empty store. And we came to New York.
